name: Ethan Cole Miller
reasoning: Has oily, clogged pores and breakouts, so a product that actually fights pimples is most useful. Prefers simple, non-greasy, fragrance-free, fast-absorbing products, clear labels, and drugstore price—would choose the option that directly addresses acne.
improvement: Make the formula matte and clearly labeled “non-comedogenic,” list a straightforward active (salicylic acid for daily use or benzoyl peroxide as a spot option), keep it fragrance-free, use simple pump/small dropper packaging that doesn’t waste product, include clear directions, and offer a small trial size.

name: Valentina Marisol Vega
gender: Female (identifies as female; comfortable answering gender on forms and shopping for gendered and gender-neutral skincare products)
occupation: Part-time Retail Beauty Specialist and Brand Ambassador; Freelance Micro-Influencer (skincare-focused)

Ideal with gentle actives (low-% AHA or tranexamic acid + niacinamide), humectant support, and a lightweight, non-pilling texture.

name: Caleb Preston Lane
gender: Male (identifies as male; typically shops straightforward product rigs and sometimes seeks gender-marketed items or minimal routines)
occupation: Freelance Content Creator / Skincare & Lifestyle Creator
reasoning: A foundational, broad-appeal product that protects the skin during active use (retinoids/acids), reduces risk of irritation that acne-specific actives can cause, encourages repeat purchases, and is content-friendly for educational demos and visible-repair stories.
improvement: Make it evidence-first: provide dermatologist endorsement or third-party clinical data, ingredient transparency (concentrations/pH), and offer samples/travel sizes for low-risk patch-testing.

name: Kai Nguyen
gender: Non-binary (uses they/them pronouns); identifies as genderqueer and prefers gender-neutral branding and inclusive product copy
occupation: Full-time undergraduate student; part-time barista/shift lead
reasoning: Barrier-first approach for very sensitive, reactive skin: repairing the barrier reduces redness, irritation, and downstream breakouts. Ingredient fit: ceramide-rich formula (ceramides + cholesterol + fatty acids) with a low-concentration niacinamide option is evidence-backed for barrier function. Practical value: fewer corrective products (good for a student budget) and safer for people who flare easily.
improvement: Provide a travel/sample size (decant) so I can do a 72-hour patch test before buying a full-size product.

name: Aaliyah Simone Thompson
gender: Female (identifies as female; typically comfortable answering gender on forms and shopping for gendered and gender-neutral skincare products)
occupation: Full-time college student; Part-time sales associate
reasoning: My main issue is inflammatory breakouts and clogged pores, so a product explicitly targeted at acne with clinically proven actives speaks to that need. I trust clinical claims over hype and want ingredient concentrations/evidence to evaluate efficacy and risk of irritation.
improvement: Offer a gentle 'starter' or buffered lower‑strength version and include a small trial/sample with clear usage guidance; pair the active with barrier‑supporting ingredients (e.g., niacinamide, ceramides, hyaluronic) and ensure fragrance‑free, non‑comedogenic, pump/airless packaging and reasonable pricing.

name: Mateo Javier Navarro
gender: Identifies as male (often prefers straightforward product rigs; sometimes responsive to gender-marketed items or minimal routines)
reasoning: Directly addresses common, high-impact problem of breakouts that can scar; clinically proven actives are trusted and a serum fits a short, consistent routine. Preference contingent on minimizing irritation (fragrance-free, non-comedogenic, lightweight for LA humidity) and on transparency of active concentrations so use can be titrated.
improvement: Add explicit concentration transparency and barrier-supporting co-ingredients (glycerin/low-weight humectants and a small amount of niacinamide or ceramide-precursors). Offer a 10–15 mL trial size and include a short how-to card: when to use (AM/PM), what not to mix (e.g., avoid direct BPO+retinoid layering), and instructions for patch testing.

name: Harper Elise Whitman

name: Ari Lin Bennett
gender: Prefers not to disclose or other gender identity, may avoid explicitly gendered marketing and choose products by ingredient lists and peer recommendations
occupation: Pharmacy Technician (part-time); occasional gig worker (delivery and on-demand retail shifts)
reasoning: Barrier-first approach is safest and most useful for sensitive, easily irritated skin; a well-formulated barrier cream (ceramide complex + cholesterol + fatty acids, humectant like glycerin or low‑MW hyaluronic, and an occlusive option) reduces redness and prevents future flares more reliably than an active-focused acne serum or potentially acidic/fragile glow toner.
improvement: Provide a small, affordable sample/travel size and publish full INCI plus the preservative system and any active concentrations (label fragrance‑free and avoid essential oils).

name: Maya Celeste Park-Garcia
gender: Identifies as female; typically comfortable answering gender on forms and shops for both gendered and gender-neutral skincare products

More useful day-to-day than an acne serum (she already uses spot treatments) and more convenient than a heavy barrier cream for rushed campus mornings.
